docs.title: MTUOC: A Practical Course on Machine Translation Technologies
docs.text:
Welcome to this open online course on practical machine translation technologies that introduces learners to the use of MTUOC, a modular ecosystem of open-source tools designed to make machine translation workflows more accessible to translators, language professionals, teachers, researchers and students. The course is organised into four modules:

  1. Deployment, Integration and Evaluation
  2. Corpus Engineering
  3. NMT Training and Fine-tuning
  4. LLMs for Translation

This course has been developed by:

  • Antoni Oliver Universitat Oberta de Catalunya aoliverg@uoc.edu

  • Sergi Alvarez-Vidal Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona sergi.alvarez@uab.cat

To begin, go to About This Course or How to Navigate This Course.

docs.title: About this Course
docs.text:
MTUOC: A Practical Course on Machine Translation Technologies is an open online course designed to introduce learners to practical machine translation technologies through the MTUOC ecosystem.

Machine translation is now part of many professional translation, localisation and multilingual communication workflows. However, many open-source machine translation and large language model tools require technical knowledge that may be unfamiliar to translators, language professionals and students in the humanities. This course addresses that gap by offering a practical, guided and accessible introduction to machine translation technologies.

The course focuses on MTUOC, a modular ecosystem of open-source tools designed to make machine translation workflows easier to deploy, evaluate, integrate and adapt. Through a set of step-by-step activities, learners will explore how machine translation systems can be used, evaluated and connected to professional translation environments.

The course is organised into four modules:

  1. Deployment, Integration and Evaluation Learners will explore how to start machine translation systems, use MTUOC Server, connect MT systems to translation workflows and evaluate translation output with automatic metrics.

  2. Corpus Engineering This module introduces the preparation, cleaning, alignment and conversion of linguistic data for machine translation purposes.

  3. NMT Training and Fine-tuning Learners will work with neural machine translation workflows, including model training, adaptation and deployment.

  4. LLMs for Translation This module introduces the use of large language models in translation-related workflows, including prompt-based translation and fine-tuning approaches.

The course is aimed at translation students, language professionals, teachers, researchers and anyone interested in understanding how machine translation technologies work in practice. No advanced programming background is required, although learners should be willing to work with digital tools, follow technical instructions and experiment with command-line environments when needed.

By the end of the course, learners will be able to:

  • understand the main components of a machine translation workflow;
  • deploy and test machine translation systems using MTUOC tools;
  • evaluate machine translation output with automatic metrics;
  • prepare and manage linguistic resources for machine translation;
  • understand the basic logic of neural machine translation training and fine-tuning;
  • explore the role of large language models in translation workflows;
  • critically reflect on the possibilities and limitations of machine translation technologies.

docs.title: Licence and reuse
docs.text:
This course is conceived as an open educational resource. Its materials are intended to be reusable, adaptable and extensible for teaching, self-learning and research training contexts.

Users may follow the full course sequence or reuse individual modules, tutorials and activities according to their own learning, teaching or training needs.

docs.title: How to Navigate This Course
docs.text: This page explains how the course is organised and how to make the most of the learning experience.

docs.title: Before You Begin
docs.text:
To complete the practical activities in this course, you should:

  • have basic computer literacy;
  • be comfortable downloading and installing software;
  • be willing to follow step-by-step technical tutorials.

No previous programming experience is required.

Some practical activities require Docker Desktop and other software components. Installation instructions are provided when they are first needed in the course.

docs.title: Course Structure
docs.text:
The course is organised into four thematic modules. Each module follows a similar structure to help you progress through the course.

A typical module includes:

  • an introduction to the topic;
  • learning objectives;
  • learning materials;
  • practical tutorials;
  • hands-on activities;
  • a self-assessment quiz;
  • additional resources, where appropriate.

The course is self-paced, but we recommend completing the modules in order, as each module builds on concepts introduced previously.

docs.title: Discord Community
docs.text:
A dedicated Discord channel is available for each module.

Use these channels to:

  • ask questions;
  • discuss the activities;
  • share useful resources;
  • exchange ideas with other participants.
Channel Purpose #general General announcements and questions about the course #module-1 Module 1 discussions #module-2 Module 2 discussions #module-3 Module 3 discussions #module-4 Module 4 discussions

docs.title: Recommended Learning Path
docs.text:
For each module, we recommend following this sequence:

  1. Read the introduction and learning objectives.
  2. Study the learning materials.
  3. Complete the practical tutorials.
  4. Carry out the proposed activities.
  5. Take the self-assessment quiz.
  6. Join the discussion on Discord.
  7. Continue to the next module.

docs.title: Self-Assessment
docs.text:
Each module concludes with a short self-assessment quiz designed to help you review the main concepts before continuing.

The quizzes are intended to support your learning. You may repeat them as many times as you wish.

docs.title: Need Help?
docs.text:
If you need assistance during the course:

  • post your question in the appropriate Discord channel;
  • contact the course authors if you need further support.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Machine translation technologies are useful when they can be deployed, connected to real translation workflows and evaluated in a reliable way. This module introduces these three practical dimensions through the MTUOC ecosystem.

You will start by looking at different ways of deploying machine translation systems. You will then learn how to start translation models with MTUOC Server, including both neural machine translation (NMT) models and LLM-based translation models. The module then moves from deployment to use: you will explore how MT systems can be integrated into translation workflows and how MTUOC Translator can be used as an interface for working with machine translation.

The final part of the module focuses on automatic evaluation. You will learn why evaluation is necessary, what automatic metrics can and cannot tell us, and how tools such as SacreBLEU and COMET can support the comparison of machine translation outputs. Automatic evaluation is presented as a practical method for obtaining reproducible, scalable results, while keeping in mind that human evaluation remains essential for assessing aspects such as nuance, adequacy and style.

By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • understand different deployment options for machine translation systems;
  • start NMT models with MTUOC Server;
  • start LLM-based translation models for translation tasks;
  • connect machine translation systems to translation workflows;
  • use MTUOC Translator as a translation interface;
  • evaluate machine translation output with automatic metrics;
  • interpret automatic evaluation results critically.

docs.title: Module Roadmap
docs.text:
This module is organised into the following sections:

  1. Deployment Introduction to the main ways in which machine translation systems can be deployed and accessed.

  2. Starting NMT Models with MTUOC Server Practical guidance on starting neural machine translation models using MTUOC Server.

  3. Starting LLM-Based Translation Models Introduction to using LLM-based systems for translation through MTUOC tools.

  4. Integration into Translation Workflows Explanation of how machine translation systems can be connected to professional translation environments and workflows.

  5. MTUOC Translator Practical use of MTUOC Translator as an interface for working with machine translation systems.

  6. Automatic Evaluation Introduction to automatic MT evaluation and the use of metrics such as SacreBLEU and COMET.

  7. Practical Activity A hands-on task to apply the main concepts and tools covered in the module.

  8. Self-Assessment A short quiz to review the key concepts before moving on to the next module.

We recommend completing the sections in the order presented.
